title: CBA rout just start of budget fallout
full_text:
If there was one stock price reaction that underscored the level of investor angst about the 2026–27 federal budget last week, it was the record fall in Commonwealth Bank.
A key question for investors now is whether the tax changes in the budget will tip CBA into the list of major blue-chips – like CSL and Cochlear – that are considered to be “ex-growth”.
If CBA’s earnings and capital growth potential are set to fade, then investors will be wanting more than the 3.3 per cent fully franked dividend yield currently on offer. Perhaps the only way they’re going to get that is if the share price falls further.
Considering its economic exposure, CBA shares were very well supported until recently.
Despite the energy crisis and rapid-fire interest rate hikes from the Reserve Bank, CBA shares were up 15 per cent by mid-April. But the biggest stock on the ASX dived 10.4 per cent on Wednesday – eclipsing its falls during the Global Financial Crisis and the Covid-19 pandemic.
It came after the government announced major changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax that threaten the residential property market and investor lending to which CBA is heavily exposed.
In a report published after that sell-off, JPMorgan analyst Andrew Triggs warned that the hangover from the federal budget’s tax announcements adds to concerns that CBA is “ex-growth”.
Triggs said CBA remained vulnerable after crashing to a three-month low of $151 last week.
His target of $129 implies CBA will drop a further 19 per cent fall from Friday’s close of $159.59.
CBA’s third-quarter cash profit of $2.7bn met expectations and margins held steady.
But regulatory capital disappointed, personal lending arrears spiked, cloud computing, software licences and AI investment costs jumped – and the budget’s tax changes will hit loan growth.
“When combined with the hangover from Tuesday night’s federal budget announcements on tax policy, for us the softer capital position stokes new-found fears that CBA is ex-growth,” Triggs said. “While this may be somewhat overstated, valuation prior to today was more compatible with a tech stock darling than a very good banking franchise in a mature slow-growing market such as Australia.” Lower capital could limit the bank’s ability to chase new lending – especially if credit quality keeps deteriorating. Wednesday’s budget tax announcements just added to such concerns.
VanEck head of investments Russel Chesler warns that bank-led leadership on the ASX may be coming to an end.
“We could be seeing the start of a regime shift,” Chesler said. “We’re in a structurally different earnings environment to the one that delivered the last five years of bank performance. Australian investors may need to look beyond the big banks to capture the next phase of opportunity on the ASX.” But there are concerns that the tax changes will have wider implications for the stockmarket, particularly for “growth” stocks owned primarily for their potential capital returns.
That is highlighted by the fact that the ASX 200 has been one of the worst performers this year.
In US dollar terms the ASX 200 is down about 1 per cent while the MSCI All Country World index is up about 10 per cent, and the MSCI Asia Pacific index is up about 20 per cent.
“This weakness is only in Australia – hitting the ASX – and is due to the federal government’s budget that screws investors in Australia,” said Bell Potter’s Richard Coppleson. “All the other major stockmarkets have been surging in the last month while we have been dropping.
“The ASX and Australia will be worse off from these changes.” Coppleson is concerned that companies with larger offshore revenues and businesses in “growth mode” will find it harder to attract local investors to buy their stock.
“These companies will have a much lower multiple – price-to-earnings – than their peers in the US, as investors here will not want to pay a 50 per cent capital gains tax. “So these companies will move to, say, the US, where capital gains taxes are so low that there are millions of investors who will buy their stock, thus attracting a much higher price-to-earnings multiple.”
summary: Commonwealth Bank's shares fell 10.4% after the federal budget announced tax changes affecting property and lending. Analysts warn this could signal a decline in the bank's growth potential, raising concerns for investors.
author: David Rogers, Exchange
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: CBA shares dropped to a three-month low of $151.
JPMorgan analyst predicts a further 19% decline to $129.
Tax changes threaten CBA's exposure to the property market.
ASX 200 underperforms compared to global markets.
Concerns about a shift in investment focus away from big banks.
ticker_line: CBA shares plunge after budget tax changes
